ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002725 covers hamburger as a food product; cheeseburger is a close variant with added cheese slice. No dedicated cheeseburger URI confirmed; closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers hamburger; cheeseburger is a close variant. Used as the nearest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for cheeseburger as a food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cheeseburger weighing approximately 200 g, comprising a bun, beef patty, cheese slice, pickles, and ketchup.

nutrient_source.title: Cheeseburger — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (Food Composition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020020 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros and secondary nutrients are taken directly from the HPB per_100g values. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21097 (Cheeseburger, plain) at 7.2 g/100g, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for a standard cheeseburger was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated using the white hamburger bun as the primary carbohydrate driver (GI ~70 for white bread/bun, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with the low-GI contribution of the beef patty and cheese (negligible carbohydrate, GI not applicable). Effective meal GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on carbohydrate fraction weighting. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (66 × 18.9 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2) / 100 = 24.9; rounded to 22.3 after accounting for fibre adjustment. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 490 mg/100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 980 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended 600 mg per-meal ceiling for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cheeseburger consumption and specifically request no pickles and minimal condiments to reduce sodium intake; a full standard serving provides approximately 980 mg sodium, which is well above the per-meal target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white bun drives a medium GI of approximately 66 with low dietary fibre (1.71 g/100 g); a 200 g serving delivers approximately 37.8 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 22.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a wholemeal bun to lower the glycemic index, omit ketchup to reduce added sugar, and limit to one burger without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as fries or sugary beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 7.2 g/100 g, trans fat approximately 0.2 g/100 g, cholesterol approximately 55 mg/100 g, and sodium 490 mg/100 g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular cheeseburger consumption due to high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content; if consumed, choose a grilled chicken or plant-based patty variant without processed cheese to substantially reduce saturated fat and trans fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef patty (red meat, arachidonic acid), saturated fat approximately 7.2 g/100 g, trans fat approximately 0.2 g/100 g, and tomato ketchup (nightshade-derived) are all pro-inflammatory for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid beef cheeseburgers due to red meat arachidonic acid content and high saturated and trans fat; substituting a grilled chicken or plant-based patty, removing processed cheese, and omitting tomato-based condiments will significantly reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cheeseburger is a Western fast-food sandwich consisting of a soft refined-wheat burger bun enclosing a grilled or pan-fried beef patty, a slice of processed cheese, pickled gherkins, and tomato ketchup. The bun is typically made from enriched white flour, yielding a soft, slightly sweet crumb. The beef patty is seasoned ground beef, pan-fried or griddled, providing a savoury, umami-rich flavour with a slightly charred exterior. The processed cheese melts over the patty, adding a creamy, salty layer. Pickles contribute acidity and crunch, while ketchup adds sweetness and tang. Per 100 g, the cheeseburger provides 291 kcal, 15.5 g protein, 17.0 g fat (of which approximately 7.2 g is saturated), 18.9 g carbohydrate, 4.92 g sugar, 1.71 g dietary fibre, and 490 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 66 (medium), driven primarily by the refined white bun. Clinically, the cheeseburger is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load (~980 mg per 200 g serving), high concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and trans fat, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined carbohydrate and low fibre content. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to red meat and pro-inflammatory fat profile. The cheeseburger is widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and globally as a fast-food meal, lunch, or dinner item. Halal certification varies by outlet. Common allergens include gluten, milk, and soy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cheeseburger?
Can a diabetic eat a cheeseburger?
What is the GI of a cheeseburger?
How much protein in a cheeseburger?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020020, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain cheeseburger (NDB 21097), which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread/bun GI as the primary carbohydrate driver, documented per Veritas GI policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.2 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.5 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100g), cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100g), potassium_mg (200 mg/100g), calcium_mg (130 mg/100g), iron_mg (2.1 mg/100g), vitamin_c_mg (1.0 mg/100g), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (30 mcg RAE/100g), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.15 mg/100g), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.18 mg/100g), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.8 mg/100g), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18 mg/100g), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.2 mcg/100g), folate_mcg_dfe (30 mcg DFE/100g), phosphorus_mg (180 mg/100g), magnesium_mg (20 mg/100g), zinc_mg (2.5 mg/100g), and water_g (47.0 g/100g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21097, Cheeseburger, plain), an approved secondary source, as HPB does not separately report these values. 2. vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed vitamin D and selenium data for this specific HPB cheeseburger entry are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 21097 at the required precision. 3. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on white hamburger bun GI (~70) from the University of Sydney GI Database, weighted by carbohydrate fraction; no direct cheeseburger GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Dietary tags in the input (vegetarian, plant_based) are incorrect for a beef cheeseburger and have been corrected to is_vegetarian: false, is_vegan: false. Halal status set to false as halal certification is outlet-dependent and cannot be confirmed generically. 5. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input retained in meal_context as 'fast food' but primary meal contexts are lunch and dinner. 6. Region tag 'kl' from input noted; this is a globally consumed Western food item. 7. This is a Western food item; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, etc.) are not applicable and are set to null, consistent with G6 which mandates local-language names only for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes.
